blocks.body: Sentence Transformations — Conditionals shows how to rewrite condition, result, regret, and strong advice through equivalent conditional patterns.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"In conditional transformations, you need to preserve the same kind of condition or speaker attitude: warning, regret, preference, strong advice, or access condition.","rules":[{"label":"UNLESS = IF NOT","color":"emerald","text":"Pairs with unless work only when the new sentence really means if not and does not create a double negative.","example":"Unless you hurry, you'll miss it."},{"label":"WISH / WOULD RATHER","color":"blue","text":"Through wish and would rather, you often rewrite regret, unreal situations, or a personal preference about another action.","example":"I wish I had studied more."},{"label":"HAD BETTER","color":"amber","text":"Had better expresses strong advice or a warning about a consequence, not just neutral advice.","example":"You had better leave now."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. How to Spot the Needed Rewrite
blocks.body:
Conditional transformations are rarely only about if. You usually need to see the function behind the sentence.

  • Unless -> check whether the original really means if not.
  • Wish / if only -> look for regret about the present or the past.
  • Would rather -> decide whether the preference is about your own action or another person's action.
  • Had better -> use it when the sentence carries a warning or an unwanted consequence.

blocks.heading: 2. Core Transformation Patterns
blocks.body:
Base sentenceTarget sentenceRule focus
If you don't hurry, you'll miss the train.Unless you hurry, you'll miss the train.if ... not -> unless.
I regret not studying harder.I wish I had studied harder.regret about the past -> wish + Past Perfect.
I'd prefer you not to call so late.I'd rather you didn't call so late.would prefer -> would rather + past form for a different subject.
You should leave now.You had better leave now.strong advice / warning -> had better.

In exam tasks, the real challenge is to keep the same degree of force and the same speaker attitude.

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Adding a second negative after unless.","wrong":"Unless you don't hurry, you'll miss the train.","right":"Unless you hurry, you'll miss the train.","hint":"Unless already contains the meaning if not."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using the wrong tense after wish.","wrong":"I wish I studied harder last year.","right":"I wish I had studied harder last year.","hint":"Regret about the past needs Past Perfect."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Adding to after had better.","wrong":"You had better to leave now.","right":"You had better leave now.","hint":"After had better, use the bare infinitive."},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"rose","title":"Using would rather with another subject but no past form.","wrong":"I'd rather you don't call late.","right":"I'd rather you didn't call late.","hint":"When the subject is different, would rather is often followed by a past form."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick Exam Check","items":["First decide whether the sentence expresses a condition, regret, preference, or strong advice.","Make sure the new form keeps the same shade of meaning.","After unless, avoid extra negation, and after had better, do not add to.","At the end, check whether the tense after wish and would rather still works correctly."]}
